June 2023

# **Purpose of the Guide**

This guide provides information, guidance and advice on job description writing for organizations within the core public administration (CPA). Definitions of terms related to job descriptions can be found in [Appendix A](#_Appendix_A:_Definitions) of this guide.

Responsibilities in relation to job descriptions are established by the Treasury Board of Canada Secretariat (TBS) in the [classification policy instruments](https://www.gcpedia.gc.ca/wiki/TBS_Classification_Program_for_the_CPA/Policy_Suite).

In addition, most collective agreements include a clause requiring managers to provide job descriptions to employees upon request.

**Principles of the Classification Program and Link to Job Descriptions**

The Classification Program established and managed by TBS sets the principles for the sound organization and measurement of work in the CPA. Those principles are listed in the table below and their links to job descriptions are described alongside.

Figure 1: Classification Program principles and links to job descriptions

<div class="joplin-table-wrapper"><table><tbody><tr><th><ol><li>Supporting the creation of a hierarchy of work to which compensation can be mapped</li></ol></th><th></th><th><p>Well-written job descriptions provide clarity on the work performed by each position at a given group and level in the organization to map compensation.<strong></strong></p></th></tr><tr><td></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><ol><li>Providing the policy framework, direction, and tools to foster the prudent management of the wage bill through appropriate classification decisions and classification relativity across the CPA; and</li></ol></td><td></td><td><p>Well-written job descriptions help determine the proper classification of the position and maintain consistent relativity. They thereby ensure that comparable positions are compensated equitably</p></td></tr><tr><td></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><ol><li>Ensuring that public funds are managed in accordance with principles of affordability and value for money outlined in the <a href="https://www.tbs-sct.canada.ca/pol/doc-eng.aspx?id=12084"><em>Policy Framework for the Management of Compensation</em></a><em> </em>and the <a href="https://www.tbs-sct.canada.ca/pol/doc-eng.aspx?id=32495"><em>Policy on Financial Management</em></a>.</li></ol><p></p></td><td></td><td><p>Well-written job descriptions strengthen and reinforce the stewardship role of the organization and the Classification Program</p></td></tr></tbody></table></div>

Job descriptions are crucial to the Classification Program in the CPA. They describe the work assigned to positions in the CPA's position-based classification system. Every employee is appointed to a specific position and the work assigned to that employee is described in a job description. The job description is used to determine the appropriate occupational group and level of the position, which in turn, sets employee compensation.

# **Job Descriptions in Relation to Other Aspects of Human Resources Management**

In addition to their importance in determining appropriate compensation, job descriptions are also the foundation for many of the human resources management functions needed to attract and retain skilled workers. This includes recruitment, performance management, talent management, learning, labour relations and official languages. For example, poorly written job descriptions can result in inappropriate classification and compensation which, in turn, can lead to employee dissatisfaction and grievances, as well as recruitment and retention difficulties.

The diagram in Figure 2 below shows some of the linkages between job descriptions and other aspects of human resources management.

Figure 2: Job description linkages

Examples of how job descriptions are used in HR management:

- Staffing: to develop a Statement of Merit Criteria (SOMC) for use in a selection process;
- Orientation: provides a starting point to better understand the employee's roles, responsibilities, and duties in relation to other positions in the organization;
- Supervision: allows the supervisor to better understand the roles, responsibilities and duties associated with each position in their team;

- Training: to identify areas where the employee requires additional knowledge or competencies, learning requirements and to prepare a training and development plan.
- Performance Management: job descriptions can serve as the basis for establishing work objectives that are used to provide feedback and evaluate employee performance.
- Compensation: to identify the corresponding salary range for the occupational group and level;

# **Roles and Responsibilities**

This section describes the roles of the stakeholders in job description writing. The main stakeholders are Managers, Organizational Design and Classification (OC) Advisors and employees.

**Managers** are responsible for:

- Obtaining advice from an OC Advisor in their organization when writing or choosing a job description for a position in their unit or when altering the duties described in a job description;
- Ensuring that job descriptions reflect the work assigned and performed by their employees;
- Determining the content of the job descriptions and ensuring it is updated when significant changes in the work occur or when new work is assigned, as required;
- Ensuring employees have an opportunity to review, comment on, and sign their job descriptions;
- Establishing a reasonable and defensible effective date; and,
- Approving and signing the final job description.

**Organizational Design and Classification Advisors** are responsible for:

- Advising managers on organizational design and job description content, taking into consideration the organizational context;
- Providing advice to managers on what information should be included in a job description to ensure it can be evaluated using the job evaluation standards;
- Providing advice and expertise on matters related to job descriptions; and
- Advising managers on the selection and use of interdepartmental standardized job descriptions and on the use, selection and drafting of standardized job descriptions.

**Employees** are responsible for:

- Discussing the content of their job description with their manager, as required.
- When applicable, providing comments on the content of their job description to their manager and/or an OC Advisor when it is being updated, and
- Signing their job description to confirm that they have had the opportunity to review the content, however they are not required to agree with it.

# **Using Standardized Job Descriptions**

A standardized job description (SJD) is a document that describes the work assigned to a job classified at a specific occupational group and level, which is common across a number of work units, regions, or organizations.

TBS supports the use of SJDs as an efficient management practice and they should be implemented whenever possible. These also ensure greater consistency and relativity, and reduce the workload for managers and OC Advisors, as SJDs minimize the need to create several unique job descriptions.

An interdepartmental standardized job description (ISJD) is a job description that has been validated by the Office of the Chief Human Resources Officer (OCHRO) and is assigned a specific OCHRO decision job number. They describe enduring work and are usually developed by functional communities (e.g., Office of the Chief Information Officer) to be applied across the CPA and to take full advantage of the benefits of standardizing job descriptions.

## Benefits

SJDs and ISJDs are essential tools that will enable our human resources system to respond more quickly to organizational business requirements by:

Streamlining the classification process by reducing the time organizations spend on drafting and evaluating individual job descriptions;

Increasing the transparency and consistency of classification decisions by ensuring identical work is evaluated the same way across organizations;

Increasing efficiency by reducing the duplication of work by not having to re-write and re-evaluate the same job descriptions or creating several unique, yet similar, job descriptions;

Supporting fair and appropriate compensation management by ensuring that identical jobs are compensated the same way in all organizations; and

Facilitating the transition to a new job evaluation standard (i.e., classification conversion).

## Writing a Standardized Job Description (SJD)

The principles in this document can also be applied when creating an SJD. A well-developed SJD accurately describes the duties of the job, reflects the work of identical positions, and describes work that is appropriate to the organizational context in which it is found.

To develop SJDs, management, in consultation with their OC Advisor, should develop functional charts that describe distinct clusters of work based on the organization's mandate. This will help the manager organize work into streams that can be used to develop SJDs.

[Appendix B](#_Appendix_B:_Standardized) provides a high-level outline of the process to create SJDs.

**Smart Practice**

Consult with an OC Advisor prior to creating an SJD.

Consult the bargaining agent and keep them informed on the development of SJDs.

**Remember** ![](data:image/png;base64,)

Just because a particular SJD works in one part of the organization, this does not mean it will work in another.

# **Writing a Job Description**

The job description should focus on the primary purpose of the work. The responsibilities of a position flow from the mandate, objectives and goals of the organizational context within which it is located. A clear link must exist between the responsibilities of the position and the organization's mandate. Care must be taken to ensure that job descriptions do not duplicate responsibilities already assigned to other jobs within the organization.

The elements found in a job description in the core public administration are aligned with _Pay Equity Act_ that states there is a requirement for employers under federal jurisdiction to consider the following criteria in assessing the value of work performed: Skill, Effort, Responsibility and Working Conditions. This requirement guides the information found in the job description, but the following principles also apply:

- **Use language that describes with equal complexity the work done by all genders**. If weaker language is used to describe work traditionally done by women or work done at lower levels of the hierarchy, it can result in the job being undervalued. It is very important to use consistent, bias-free language when writing job descriptions for all types of work.
- **Use language that describes physical aspects of the work in the most inclusive terms possible.** While some jobs do have specific bona fide physical requirements, many important elements of jobs can be performed regardless of physical ability or disability. Be careful to consider the true nature of effort involved in performing tasks. For instance, the effort involved in attending meetings is more related to remaining stationary during the meeting than to being seated. By using more inclusive language in this situation, you can capture the true effort required to attend meetings for employees of all physical circumstances.
- **Include all significant aspects of the work to ensure it can be properly evaluated.** Consult with an OC Advisor to ensure that all of the information required to evaluate the job has been incorporated in the job description.

At the outset, the manager should consult an OC Advisor to get advice and guidance on the job description writing process.

For more information on job description writing, consult the online course "Introduction to Organization and Classification (COR133)" offered by the Canada School of Public Service. The module on job descriptions provides additional tips and guidance related to job description writing.

<div class="joplin-table-wrapper"><table><tbody><tr><th><p><strong>Smart practice: preparing to write job descriptions</strong></p><ul><li>Contact your OC Advisor for advice and guidance.</li><li>Use an ISJD or SJD whenever possible.</li><li>Use the <a href="#_Appendix_E:_Blank">Job Description Template</a> if you need to develop a job description.</li><li>Review the requirements of the position with other managers and the employee(s) performing the work to ensure you capture all the significant aspects of the work and there is no overlap with other jobs in the organization.</li><li>Consult with the organization's functional authorities of corporate functions, such as information technology, finance, communications, and human resources in order to obtain their input when the work touches on these mandates.</li></ul></th></tr></tbody></table></div>

# **Completing the Job Description Template**

The [job description template (Appendix F)](#_Appendix_E:_Blank) contains a number of sections. The sections used to describe work are those outlined in the [_Pay_](https://www.laws-lois.justice.gc.ca/eng/acts/p-4.2/page-1.html) _Equity Act,_ being Skill, Effort, Responsibility and Working Conditions.

A job description is intended to describe the ongoing and current requirements of the job. It is not meant to describe work that will be assigned in the future.

It is important to keep in mind that a job description describes the work activities carried out by the employee and is not related to how well the employee accomplishes the work activities. Employee job performance is measured separately through the public service performance management cycle.

More specific tips on writing job descriptions can be found in [Appendix C](#_Appendix_C:_Writing) and [Appendix D](#_Appendix_D:_Tips) of this guide. An example of a completed job description can be found in [Appendix E](#_Appendix_E:_Example).

For greater clarity, you will find below more details on the requirements for each section of the job description template.

## Position Information

This section captures identifying information on the specific position that is linked to the job. Every job description must be signed and dated by the manager and include the following information:

- Position number and title;
- Group, sub-group, and level;
- Effective date;
- Organization;
- Branch/Division;
- Geographic location;
- Job/Standardized job number;
- Supervisor's position number, title, group, sub-group, and level;
- National Occupational Classification code; and
- Language, security, and communication requirements.

**Smart Practice: Job description approval**

Job descriptions must be signed and dated by the responsible manager before being submitted for evaluation. By signing the job description, the manager is attesting that it is an accurate description of the duties assigned.

Job descriptions should be signed by the employee to indicate that the person has had the opportunity to read and comment on the content of the job description. An employee's signature does not mean that the person agrees with the content of the job description.

## Client Service Results

Client Service Results summarize the primary purpose of the job. They describe the products and/or services that a job provides or delivers. They are the results of the work activities, but do not describe the activities themselves. Describing these results sets the tone for the rest of the job description.

**Example 1: Records Clerk**

Within the records office, provides records support and coordination services to employees\* of the organization. (\*includes all staff up to and including senior managers)

**Example 2: Human Resources Assistant (Generalist**)

Working within a human resources (HR) unit, the HR Assistant provides and coordinates HR administrative support services in two or more HR disciplines to colleagues and clients, which include employees and managers of the organization.

## Key Activities

Key activities represent a high-level summary of what is accomplished by the job in order to provide the services and/or products listed in the Client Service Results. They should be clear, precise, and concise. Additional details on the activity should be explained in the appropriate section of the job description.

When considering what responsibilities of the job to include, do not consider the percentage of time spent on that activity. It is not a reliable indicator of significance. Instead consider the importance of that activity in the context of delivering on the primary purpose, or client service result.

Answering the following questions can help formulate key activity statements:

- What is the most important work being done?
- What responsibilities belong to this job?

**Example Key Activities for a Records Clerk:**

- Analyses and classifies records; assigns metadata; creates document profiles; creates or revises subject or series titles and recommends modifications to the subject file classification system structures.
- Conducts quality control monitoring and analysis of records including replacing and repairing damaged files, and retrieves records identified for destruction according to disposal schedule.

Normally, five to seven key activities are enough to describe the core functions of the job.

## Skills

This section describes the skills (both intellectual and physical) and the knowledge required to deliver on the responsibilities and achieve the primary purpose of the job.

Physical skills include sensory and physical skill. When writing this factor ensure that it is not simply a repetition of the physical effort and working conditions sections and take care to describe both physical and sensory effort in the most inclusive terms possible. For more information on this, please refer to Appendix D.

**Examples of Physical Skill:**

- Physical dexterity, coordination, timing, and precision are required to operate, adjust, assemble, and dismantle systems, equipment, and machinery. This includes the precision machining of replacement parts for equipment. This skill requires extensive practice to maintain proficiency.
- The work requires mental and visual concentration in detecting and diagnosing faults, in dismantling working components and reassembling in accordance with manufacturers specifications; in calibrating engines; in fabricating parts; and in reading fuel and temperature levels.

In describing the critical thinking challenges involved in the work, it is important to consider the freedom and authority the position has to make decisions.

This section should describe:

- Areas of related knowledge, skill or expertise required to do the work;
- The judgement, initiative and discretion required to identify and resolve problems;
- The availability of direction through precedents, policies, regulations, or other employees;
- The difficulty of predicting the implications of possible courses of action;
- The impact of decisions and recommendations in terms of the effect these have on the organization, the work of others, program delivery, efficiency and effectiveness, and the significance of any precedents established;
- Specialized knowledge and the requirement to keep up with trends and developments in a particular field of expertise. It is important to indicate what knowledge is required and how it is used in relation to the key activities;
- Communications skills. Indicate who is contacted, internal and external to the organization, and explain the purpose of the contact, including the level of authority given to the subject position to reach consensus on problems. Only include contacts that are a continual and substantive responsibility of the work. Indicate the particular skills (e.g., verbal, writing, negotiation, persuasion, instructional, etc.) that are required along with the purpose for the communication;
- The physical and/or sensory skills required, indicating which work activities require these skills; and
- The level of proficiency required to perform the activities.

If it will add clarity, specific work examples can be included.

**Examples**

The examples below illustrate how to describe the skills and knowledge needed and link them to how they are used.

| **WHAT KNOWLEDGE**                                                                                                                         |     | **HOW IT IS USED**                                                                                                                                 |
| ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ | --- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
|                                                                                                                                            |     |                                                                                                                                                    |
| **Administrative Officer**<br><br>Knowledge of new administrative work methods and procedures                                              |     | In order to make changes that improve the effectiveness and efficiency of the directorate.                                                         |
|                                                                                                                                            |     |                                                                                                                                                    |
| **Senior Financial Officer**<br><br>Knowledge of financial methods, procedures, directives, guidelines and system design and configuration |     | In order to provide advice and guidance to senior managers and financial officers on the interpretation and application of legislation and policy. |

| **Policy Analyst**<br><br>Monitors, analyzes, and synthesizes economic, socio-economic, and statistical qualitative and quantitative data |     | In order to develop comprehensive policy recommendations. This involves consulting with other government organizations, provincial/territorial governments, and stakeholders. |
| ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | --- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |

## Effort

This section focuses on sensory and physical effort.

Physical effort describes the physical exertion required including physical movements and working positions.

Sensory effort describes activities and effort related to the use of any of the five senses (taste, hearing, sight, smell, touch).

The type of effort should be included as well as a description of how either the physical or mental fatigue experienced is a direct result of the effort. Any exertion required by the job that would go beyond the requirements of standard office work, including the intensity and duration of the effort, should also be described.

Describe these efforts in the plainest language possible while avoiding making narrow assumptions about the physical ability required to complete a task. For instance, sensory effort is required to focus while reviewing documents. This sensory effort is required whether it is visual or whether the document is reviewed in Braille or through a screen-reader.

This section should describe:

- The type of effort required (e.g., standing, reaching, climbing, detecting odours);
- Any work that limits physical movement;
- Duration of the effort; and
- Frequency of the effort.

**Examples**

- The work requires standing, bending, stooping, and working in cramped and awkward positions for prolonged periods of time each day to perform vehicle and equipment maintenance and repairs.
- Sensory effort is required to operate flight controls of an aircraft by manipulating them with hands and feet while observing dense ship traffic and flying the aircraft at low altitudes in an attempt to investigate ship pollution or ice hazards.
- The work typically requires physical activities such as remaining stationary while attending meetings and working on a computer. There is a requirement to transport files, boxes, equipment, or other objects.

## Responsibility

This section describes the job's responsibility for human, financial and technical resources.

This section should describe:

- The specific role, authorities and latitude for decision making related to these resources;
- Any direct supervision of employees;
- The requirement to lead working groups and project teams; and
- The level of risk involved in management of the resources.

**Examples**

- Guides new staff, temporary help, and students on administrative work practices, procedures, and processes within own work unit and on organizational administrative, human resources, and financial policies and procedures.
- Manages staff by establishing objectives, priorities, and work plans; allocating work; evaluating staff performance; identifying training needs; providing advice and direction to staff; identifying human resource requirements; responding to grievances, if identified, as part of the organizational grievance process; and participating in staffing actions.
- Manages the program's operating budget plans, forecasts, and allocates all financial resources in accordance with relevant portions of _Financial Administration Act_, _Government Contract Regulations_ and delegated financial authorities. This involves assessing the risk that contracts may publicly be scrutinized under the Access to Information Act.
- Maintains electronic and paper documents and files used by unit staff for consultation.

## Working Conditions

When describing the work environment, include only normal conditions under which the work is performed. Describe both physical work conditions, such as noise, dirt and temperature, as well as psychological conditions, such as physical danger, exposure to distressing situations, isolation and boredom and monotony. Include the duration and severity or intensity of the condition and the degree to which the employee has control over the conditions.

Do not include the requirement to work overtime or for shift work, as these are part of the conditions of employment for which employees are compensated.

**Examples**

- Works under multiple and changing priorities, deadlines and competing demands.
- Works in a records office environment with regular exposure to dust and dirt from files and frequent interruptions from clients.

# **Implementation**

When implementing any existing job description, including an interdepartmental standardized job description (ISJD) or standardized job description (SJD), pay particular attention to the organizational context within which it will be applied. Misapplication occurs when job descriptions are applied outside of the organizational context in which they were evaluated. As a result, the ratings may be impacted. This in turn may affect the level of the job.

Job descriptions should be reviewed as necessary to ensure that they are current and that organizational structures are accurate. This is particularly important when using SJDs.

**Remember** ![](data:image/png;base64,)

When the work changes significantly, the changes must be captured in a revised job description.

# **Establishing Effective Dates**

The effective date establishes when the work has officially been assigned. Managers are responsible for establishing effective dates that are reasonable and evidence based.

When the job is new, the effective date reflects the point at which the work has been officially evaluated.

In the case of a reclassification, the effective date will be established by considering what additional work resulted in the difference in group allocation and/or the change in level and when that work was assigned by the manager. In some situations, employees may be entitled to retroactive pay. Therefore, as a best practice, when a reclassification is being proposed, the manager is encouraged to discuss the effective date with the OC Advisor and employee(s). These discussions could add important information, simplify decisions, avoid disputes and possibly grievances.

Refer to the [Directive on Classification](http://intranet.canada.ca/pol/doc-eng.aspx?id=28700) for more information on establishing effective dates.

**Smart Practice: Establishing effective dates**

The OC Advisor and the employee are consulted prior to establishing an effective date when a job is proposed for reclassification.

Consult the organization's Delegation of human resources authorities document or Instrument of Delegation to obtain direction on the level of approval required in your organization for effective dates carrying a long retroactivity period (i.e., 6 months or more).

# **Maintaining Job Descriptions**

Over time, the duties of a job may change as organizational requirements shift, through the introduction of new programs or technology, or the assignment of new responsibilities by the manager. It is the manager's responsibility to ensure the continuing accuracy of a job description when significant changes occur or when new work is assigned and to update the job description accordingly. This must be done in a reasonable timeframe.

When job descriptions are not updated regularly, they may be inaccurate and no longer reflect the roles, responsibilities, and duties of the position. This could lead to an incorrect classification level and employee dissatisfaction. Not having an up-to-date job description can complicate the development of Statements of Merit Criteria (SOMC) and make it harder to develop work objectives, learning plans and to recruit the right person for the job.

Keeping job descriptions up to date is also important for matters related to recourse. Jurisprudence over the past years has established that the task of the classification grievance committee is to accept the job description and determine its appropriate classification as it is drafted. It is therefore not possible in the context of a classification grievance to modify or ignore statements in the job description even if the committee finds the activities described in the job description are not performed in their entirety.

**Smart Practice: Review and Update of Job Descriptions**

Ensure a process is in place to maintain accurate organizational structures and current job descriptions by reviewing them as necessary.

# **Enquiries**

Questions about this guide should be directed to your organizational corporate classification group. If required, they will forward questions to the [Workforce Organization and Classification Directorate](mailto:ZZORGCLA@tbs-sct.gc.ca.) at the Office of the Chief Human Resources Officer.

# **Appendix A: Definitions**

**Job Description (JD):** A document approved by the responsible manager, which describes the work requirements of a position or a job. A job description contains all the information needed to evaluate the work using the appropriate job evaluation standard.

**Position:** A specific set of duties and responsibilities outlined in a job description, to be performed by one individual in a specific organizational context.

**Job:** A collection of duties assigned by management and described in a job description.

**Job code**: The unique identifier in the human resources information management system assigned to the classification evaluation results of a job. Also known as job number or decision number.

**Standardized Job Description (SJD):** A document that describes a job applied to more than one position where all positions are classified at the same occupational group and level.

**Interdepartmental Standardized Job Descriptions (ISJD):** a document similar to an SJD, but that describes enduring work, has also been validated by the OCHRO for use across all organizations in the CPA and has been assigned a specific OCHRO decision job number.

# **Appendix B: Standardized Job Descriptions (SJD) Development Outline**

| **Timeframe\***               | **Activities**                                  |
| ----------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------- |
| Year 1                        | Phase I: Planning, Governance and Data Analysis |
| Phase II: Development of SJDs |
| Year 2                        | Phase III: Evaluation                           |
| Phase IV: Implementation      |
| As required                   | Ongoing maintenance                             |

\*Timeframes are approximate and will vary depending on project scope (number of SJDs) and resources available.

**Roles and Responsibilities**

- The Treasury Board of Canada Secretariat is responsible for determining and maintaining the occupational group structure, job evaluation tools, and people management initiatives, requirements, data, and services needed to support a high-performing public service.
- Heads of HR are responsible for ensuring that standardized and unique job descriptions are used appropriately.
- The responsibility to develop SJDs can be shared between the OC Advisor and management, but in general, these are the respective roles and responsibilities:
- The role of the Organizational Design and Classification (OC) Advisor is to:
  - ensure alignment with classification policies and directives;
  - provide advice and guidance on the process, provide feedback on the content and format of the SJD;
  - evaluate the SJD and prepare supporting documentation; and
  - support the implementation in the organization.
- The role of the manager is to:
  - provide subject-matter expertise on the work being described in an SJD;
  - implement standardized job descriptions wherever possible;
  - consult with an OC Advisor, as required, to obtain advice on organizational design, the content and format of SJDs and the application of SJDs within their organizational context; and
  - establish reasonable and evidence-based effective dates.

**Breakdown of activities**

Phase I: Planning, Governance and Data Analysis

- Identify the need and the scope of the project and engage stakeholders (i.e., bargaining agent, functional authority, business areas;)
- Develop overall project and change management plan;
- Establish project governance; and
- Analyze organizational structures to identify work streams and job descriptions to be developed.

Phase II: Development of Standardized Job Descriptions

- Gather, review, and select existing job descriptions that best describe the duties and responsibilities of each job to be described;
- Consolidate the information gathered, draft the SJDs, and the application criteria to describe the organizational context in which the SJD is to be applied (e.g[. parameters for application of PE generic products](https://www.gcpedia.gc.ca/wiki/PE_Generics_/Products));
- Confirm content by consulting with the bargaining agent, functional authority, business areas)
- Revise SJDs as required;
- Identify potential implementation issues related to organizational design and change management and determine possible strategies to address them; and
- Finalize SJDs and application criteria.

Phase III: Evaluation

- Evaluate the SJDs through a Job Evaluation Committee; and
- Prepare and finalize supporting documentation.

Phase IV: Implementation

- Ensure translation and publication of the SJDs and job evaluation rationales;
- Assign a job code to each of the job descriptions;
- Announce the availability of the products and promote their use.

Ongoing Maintenance

- Manage changes to the SJDs (e.g., review, revise, and delete) as required.

# **Appendix C: Writing Tips**

- Use clear, straightforward terms. For example, use the word "after" instead of the word "subsequently".
- Use short, concise sentences so the reader can understand. If possible, limit each sentence to a single main idea. Avoid repetition.
- Avoid unnecessary words. For example, use "modify procedures" instead of "modify existing procedures".
- Avoid ambiguous terms, limit terminology and jargon as it may cause confusion or misinterpretation and could result in the misapplication of the job evaluation standard.
- Minimize or avoid the use of acronyms. If one is required to streamline the text, write out the name in full and then indicate the acronym in parentheses. For example, the Office of the Chief Human Resources Officer (OCHRO).
- Always use the same word for the same concept. For example, when referring to "compensation" always use the word "compensation" instead of using "salary" or "rates of pay" to also mean "compensation."
- Use the active voice. Write "conduct studies" instead of "studies are conducted."
- Link ideas to each other. Write "determines and identifies search strategies and evaluates sources of information to locate records requested by clients" instead of "determines and identifies search strategies and evaluates sources of information. Locates records requested by clients."
- Use gender-inclusive language. Take care to ensure that job facts are not worded in such a way that they apply, directly or indirectly, to only men, only women, or only people of other genders.
- Do not use negative, discriminatory or stereotypical terms that would be offensive to a group.
- Use inclusive language wherever possible, particularly when describing necessary physical and sensory effort. For tips on inclusive language, please consult [Appendix D](#_Appendix_C:_Standardized).
- Avoid inflating language that makes work appear to be more demanding or more complex.
- Do not make assumptions about work being important or unimportant to the job. Describe the requirements and characteristics of the job.
- Update the job description when work has changed, as delays in documenting significant changes in the job description may lead to an incorrect classification and possible grievances.
- Describe the requirements of the work that has been assigned by the manager and not the employee's performance nor the employee's qualifications.
- Include the appropriate information that would be required to determine or to apply the appropriate job evaluation standard.
- Make sure job information is consistent with the organizational mandate and / or structure.
- Ensure the job title reflects the nature of the work and do not assume it is sufficient to provide an understanding of what is involved in the work.
- Do not write to the occupational group and level that may be expected. Write a job description that accurately describes the work.

# **Appendix D: Tips and Resources on Inclusive language**

Inclusive writing promotes equity and the equal participation of all members in society. It is writing that promotes inclusion and respect for diversity and is free of discrimination based on sex, gender, sexual orientation, race, ethnicity, disability or any other identity factor.

When describing physical skill, physical effort and sensory effort it is important to describe the requirements of the job in the broadest and most inclusive terms possible. While some jobs do have very specific bona fide physical requirements, many jobs can be accomplished by employees with a wide range of physical abilities.

Consider the true effort required and whether a very specific or narrow physical or sensory description is required. Wherever appropriate, use inclusive formulations to avoid inadvertently discriminatory language.

| Instead of…                                                                                                                           | Try                                                                                                                                   |
| ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Physical effort is required when **sitting** for extended periods of time to review, edit and develop documents or to attend meetings | The work typically requires **remaining stationary** for extended periods to review, edit and develop documents or to attend meetings |
| Physical effort is required to operate or to **lift and carry** a variety of equipment during meetings and conferences.               | Operation and transportation of a variety of equipment is required during meetings and conferences.                                   |
| **Visual** effort is required when **reading** documents and identifying discrepancies                                                | **Sensory** effort is required when **reviewing** documents and identifying discrepancies.                                            |

Other resources:

The Translation Bureau's Language Portal of Canada has produced [inclusive writing guidelines and tools](https://www.noslangues-ourlanguages.gc.ca/en/writing-tips-plus/inclusive-writing-guidelines-resources) which provide advice on ensuring documents are inclusive with respect to gender. Many of the principles they share can be applied to job description writing.

The Translation Bureau has also produced a [glossary](https://www.btb.termiumplus.gc.ca/publications/accessibilite-accessibility-eng.html) which may be consulted when seeking specific terminology on accessibility.

# **Appendix E: Example of a Completed Job Description**

**Part 1: Position information and signatures**

| **Position number**                            |                          |
| ---------------------------------------------- | ------------------------ |
| **Position title**                             | Administrative Assistant |
| **Position classification**                    | AS-01                    |
| **Position Effective date**                    |                          |
| **Job Code**                                   | GCAS010001               |
| **National occupational classification**       | 13100                    |
| **Department/Agency Name**                     |                          |
| **Geographic location**                        |                          |
| **Organizational component (Branch/Division)** |                          |
| **Office code**                                |                          |
| **Language requirements**                      |                          |
| **Linguistic profile**                         |                          |
| **Communications requirements**                |                          |
| **Security requirements**                      |                          |
| **Supervisor position number**                 |                          |
| **Supervisor position title**                  |                          |
| **Supervisor classification**                  |                          |

**Employee statement**

I have been given the opportunity to read and comment on the content of this job description.

**Employee name**:

**Employee signature**:

**Date**:

**Supervisor statement**

I certify, in compliance with the [Values and Ethics Code for the Public Sector](https://www.tbs-sct.canada.ca/pol/doc-eng.aspx?id=25049), that this job description accurately describes the work assigned to this position.

**Supervisor name**:

**Supervisor signature**:

**Date**:

**Manager authorization**

I certify, in compliance with the [Values and Ethics Code for the Public Sector](https://www.tbs-sct.canada.ca/pol/doc-eng.aspx?id=25049), that this job description accurately describes the work assigned to this position.

**Manager name**:

**Manager signature**:

**Date**:

**Part 2: Job description**

**Organizational context**

This position:

- applies to various organizations.
- resides within various structures.
- reports to various positions.
- No ongoing supervision.

**Client service results**

The provision of administrative services and support in one or more administrative fields to an assigned group within an organization.

_Administrative services include the provision of administrative support services in the following fields: human resources, finance, materiel management, security, occupational health and services, information management, procurement, facilities, accommodation and business planning._

**Key activities**

- Prepares and processes administrative requests and provides support services and guidance to management and employees on related administrative procedures and processes.
- Updates and maintains internal tracking systems and external products with key administrative information by validating information and data, identifying, and resolving discrepancies, and bringing variances and critical issues to the attention of the supervisor. Monitors the status of administrative requests and follows up with functional specialists.
- Researches, gathers, manipulates, prepares information, and drafts documents on human resources, finance, and other administrative processes to address requests and reporting requirements.
- Coordinates logistics for meetings and conferences. Prepares agendas and related documents and makes logistical arrangements.
- Contributes to the improvement of administrative processes and procedures for the assigned group.

**Skills**

Knowledge of administrative practices and procedures is required to deliver services and provide information; to validate and track information and data, process requests; to prepare information and identify the appropriate presentation format; and to contribute to administrative projects.

Knowledge of the organizational structure, mandate, roles, responsibilities, policies, directives, priorities, and systems of the organization is required in order to review administrative requests and provide administrative support; maintain current information and data on the status of administrative actions; liaise with functional specialists; coordinate meetings and conferences; and prepare appropriate information to contribute to internal reporting.

Knowledge of information management practices and techniques and database administration is required in order to maintain information and data in various organizational databases and internal tracking systems; to validate information, identify and resolve discrepancies; and to prepare, track and reconcile various administrative documents.

Knowledge of acts, legislation, regulations, and policies relevant to the field(s) of work is required in order to research, apply, and provide information to management and employees on administrative requirements; to determine options available to address needs; to make logistical arrangements for conferences and meetings; and to liaise with functional specialists to resolve issues.

Analytical skill is required in order to validate administrative information and identify and resolve discrepancies; to analyze and prioritize requests; to research, collate and format information in the appropriate format for presentation; to identify critical issues to raise to management; to contribute to administrative projects; and to reconcile information in various databases and tracking systems.

Communication skills are required to receive, interpret, and process administrative service requests; to exchange information with management, employees, and functional specialists on administrative requests; to update and maintain information systems and databases; to collate information and produce reports in appropriate formats; to coordinate meetings and conferences; and to draft correspondence related to administrative requests.

**Effort**

The work typically requires physical activities such as remaining stationary while attending meetings and working on a computer. Frequency for individual activities varies depending upon daily activities. There may be a requirement to transport files, boxes, equipment, or other objects.

The work requires sustained attention when reviewing, analyzing, and interpreting documents, reports, and data. There are opportunities to shift activity to relieve sensory strain depending upon daily activities.

**Responsibilities**

Operates, maintains, and cares for a personal computer, related software, and peripherals for the performance of own duties. Also has custody of office furniture for own use.

Applies information management (IM) processes and ensures own compliance to organization and central agency IM requirements.

Verifies that funds are available for travel, hospitality, goods and services contracts, and other expenditures in accordance with the relevant provisions of the Financial Administration Act (FAA).

Purchases goods on behalf of the organization using an acquisition card and with management approval. Makes recommendations on best value and cost. Reviews invoices and recommends payment or identifies and determines the cause of discrepancies.

Monitors, verifies and tracks recurring and ad hoc financial and administrative documents and reports; identifies variances and resolves straightforward discrepancies; raises critical or complex issues to supervisor.

Contributes to project teams or working groups on the improvement of administrative processes and procedures. Guides new employees, temporary help, and students on administrative practices, procedures, and processes.

**Working conditions**

The work is performed in a workspace with daily exposure to frequent interruptions, glare of computer screens, distractions, and ambient noise.

The work involves shifting priorities, multiple concurrent conflicting demands, and unpredictable workloads.

# **Appendix F: Blank Job Description Template**

**![](data:image/x-emf;base64,)**

**Job Description Template**

**Part 1: Position information and signatures**

| **Position number**                            |     |
| ---------------------------------------------- | --- |
| **Position title**                             |     |
| **Position classification**                    |     |
| **Position Effective date**                    |     |
| **Job Code**                                   |     |
| **National occupational classification**       |     |
| **Department/Agency Name**                     |     |
| **Geographic location**                        |     |
| **Organizational component (Branch/Division)** |     |
| **Office code**                                |     |
| **Language requirements**                      |     |
| **Linguistic profile**                         |     |
| **Communications requirements**                |     |
| **Security requirements**                      |     |
| **Supervisor position number**                 |     |
| **Supervisor position title**                  |     |
| **Supervisor classification**                  |     |

**Employee statement**

I have been given the opportunity to read and comment on the content of this job description.

**Employee name**:

**Employee signature**:

**Date**:

**Supervisor statement**

I certify, in compliance with the [Values and Ethics Code for the Public Sector](https://www.tbs-sct.canada.ca/pol/doc-eng.aspx?id=25049), that this job description accurately describes the work assigned to this position.

**Supervisor name**:

**Supervisor signature**:

**Date**:

**Manager authorization**

I certify, in compliance with the [Values and Ethics Code for the Public Sector](https://www.tbs-sct.canada.ca/pol/doc-eng.aspx?id=25049), that this job description accurately describes the work assigned to this position.

**Manager name**:

**Manager signature**:

**Date**:

**Part 2: Job description**

Add subheadings to each heading as required.

**Organizational context**

This position:

- applies to the \[add a specific work stream, if using a departmental Standardized Job Description (SJD)\].
- resides within \[add one of the following examples: the specific directorate name / the specific region name / nationally / under a specific functional authority name only / any other organization or context type not listed\].
- reports to a position \[add one of the following examples: at the xx-xx group and level / to an EX-xx position / to a supervision position at a minimum xx-xx group and level / any other position type not listed\].
- \[Add any other contextual information that should be taken into consideration when determining the application of this job description.\]

**Client service results**

**Key activities**

**Skills**

**Effort**

**Responsibilities**

**Working conditions**
